sanskrit_devanagari:
इच्छाद्वेषसमुत्थेन द्वन्द्वमोहेन भारत ।
सर्वभूतानि सम्मोहं सर्गे यान्ति परन्तप ॥ २७ ॥

translations.verbatim_quote: O scion of Bharata, O conqueror of the foe, all living entities are born into delusion, bewildered by dualities arisen from desire and hate.
O scion of Bharata, O conqueror of the foe, all living entities are born into delusion, bewildered by dualities arisen from desire and hate.

commentaries.verbatim_excerpt_status: captured (fair-use excerpt; full purport length: 1227 chars)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: The real constitutional position of the living entity is that of subordination to the Supreme Lord, who is pure knowledge. When one is deluded into separation from this pure knowledge, he becomes controlled by the illusory energy and cannot understand the Supreme Personality of Godhead.

commentaries.translator: Swami Gambhirananda (Advaita Ashrama edition of Sankara-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.27 Iccha-dvesa-samutthena, by what arises from likes and dislikes: iccha, likes, and dvesa, dislikes, are iccha-dvesau; anything arising from them is icchadvesa-samutthah. (Creatures are duluded) by that.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-27-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Adi Shankaracharya of Advaita Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: By what arises (samutthena) from likes (icchā) and dislikes (dveṣa) — that is, by the delusion of duality (dvandva-moha) — beings are deluded as soon as they enter creation. The likes and dislikes are mutually opposed pairs (like heat and cold) that capture and obscure the seeing.

commentaries.translator: Swami Adidevananda (Sri Ramakrishna Math edition of Ramanuja's Gita-bhasya, as reproduced on bhagavad-gita.us)
commentaries.verbatim_excerpt: 7.27 As soon as beings are born they are deluded. This delusion springs from sense experiences described as pairs of opposites like heat and cold. Such reactions spring from desire and hate.
commentaries.raw_full_path: data/sources/raw/bg-7-27-bgus.json (key: Commentary by Sri Ramanuja of Sri Sampradaya:)
commentaries.summary_paraphrase: Beings are deluded from the moment they are born. The delusion springs from sense-experiences described as pairs of opposites (heat/cold, pleasure/pain) which themselves spring from desire-and-hatred. Past-life vāsanā impressions manifest as instinctive desire-aversion patterns that obscure true vision.

disagreements_among_translators.explanation: All readings agree that the dualities arise from desire-and-aversion (icchā-dveṣa) and that the delusion captures all beings from the moment of entry into creation. Shankara analyses the structural mechanism (mutually-opposed pairs); Ramanuja analyses the temporal mechanism (vāsanā-impressions from prior lives). The engineering analog stays at the structural mechanism — the engineer's like/dislike captures and obscures clean perception — and is STRONG (less STRETCHED than 7.24-7.26). Coheres with Ch 2's sthita-prajña.

literal_meaning: By delusion of dualities arising from desire-and-aversion, all beings in creation come into delusion.
traditional_meaning_consensus: icchā-dveṣa-samutthena dvandva-mohena bhārata / sarva-bhūtāni sammohaṁ sarge yānti paran-tapa — by the delusion of dualities (dvandva-moha) arising (samutthena) from desire-and-aversion (icchā-dveṣa), all beings (sarva-bhūtāni) in creation (sarge) come into delusion (sammohaṁ yānti). Shankara: iccha and dveṣa, mutually opposed like heat and cold, produce a delusion of pairs that captures all beings as soon as they are born. Ramanuja: as soon as beings are born they are deluded by past-life vāsanā-impressions of pleasure-pain dualities; the subtle impressions manifest as instinctive desire-aversion patterns that obscure the true seeing.

remaining_gaps: Madhva, Abhinavagupta, Sridhara Swami, Kesava Kashmiri commentaries available in raw/ but not pulled into the source pack.
